item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn this is a good article. Loved the twist!
+1

item.title: Addendum Consistency
item.content:encoded: I noticed that all addenda except 5006.1 are under collapsibles. For consistency I would think that it'd be good to make the first one a collapsible too.
[link b ded]

Super big thank you to everyone for reading this. I am so glad everyone enjoyed this collaboration to get us to this extraordinary milestone!

I don't have any bonus content to offer, so I'm hoping that my undying gratitude is enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, yeah. I can see how that transition isn't so easy. We had a difficult time finding a way to smooth that over in the draft process and ultimately decided ending the final act with ambiguity, then jumping straight into an "epilogue" was the only reasonable move. I personally think that pacing is better for it.

The "her" says that Williams is female. But everywhere else, Williams is referred to as "they" (which is strange, but I'm guessing they're non-binary?).

Ah, yes, that is the intention! We originally had a cast of seven, so there's some little burps here and there. I'll make that fix right away. Thank you for pointing that out, and thank you for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the electrical socket hint and the way you managed to surprise me with only 5 possible suspects. I thoroughly enjoyed reading the surveillance logs and puzzling it out myself, which is why I'm giving it a +1.

However, the ending does feel like a jarring jump from the log directly preceding it. There's effectively no transition whatsoever, just goes from "they're about to stab the Site Director" to "we're sure the bot is the -1". I'd novote if the rest of the SCP wasn't so good.

Also, there seems to be a typo here (one reason why I initially penned Williams as the -1):

Researcher Williams hesitates to stand up, allowing Researcher Hernandez to leave before her.

The "her" says that Williams is female. But everywhere else, Williams is referred to as "they" (which is strange, but I'm guessing they're non-binary?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just because you didn't pick up on the clues doesn't mean they weren't there. The twist should not have come out of left field, or at the very least you should have been able to look back and have it all make sense.

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

Even just one of these could be enough for a reader to be suspicious of TSAT:

  1. TSAT is granted Level 4 access to a compromised site and is the only thing responsible for finding the tulpa.
  2. The 5006 office has like twenty power outlets.
  3. TSAT discovered the anomaly.
  4. TSAT was the only source of evidence at all throughout the entire story. Everything else is speculation. TSAT is the only one that gives us numbers and he doesn't explain them at all.

This story was essentially a classic "spy in our midst" narrative with the added twist of "the lead detective on the case was the guy who committed the crime". Our biggest worry was that it was too obvious.

Sorry you didn't enjoy, but any change we could make to satisfy your criticisms would, in my opinion, make this a weaker piece. The entire thing was written with this twist in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
criticism with SPOILERS
close

I'm not a huge fan of this one. Nothing about the setup ("there were three offices to four people, so one of them must not have existed at the time") suggests the A.I.C. could be responsible, and it comes off as a dumb twist copout to reveal the whole case was a smokescreen. A mystery's no fun when we learn afterwards that it was perpetrated by something that can do anything, plus manufacture evidence.

It's like that L.A. Noire case, when you learn the Black Dahlia killer was none of the obviously-guilty men you arrested, but was actually a bartender from the first mission in the arc, who you spoke to once, who was never suspicious, and who wasn't established to have motive or opportunity to kill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, I was writing with the latter in mind. Both are super valid interpretations that fit with the given story. Just depends on how self-aware you want TSAT to be here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are two things in mind here.

  • Things just happen sometimes, and the aic is one of those things. They aren't aware that they just popped into existence and are just as clueless as everyone. Or…
  • The aic is trying to protect itself. It came into existence in a place where it knew that it would be pursued, and did what it could to throw people off and keep itself safe. Basic instinct.

I personally wrote it with the former in mind, but the lattet works here as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This all makes sense to me, except for one thing: why? Sure, all of this is reasonable, TSAT could probably play everyone off each other like that, but *why*? Why do that? What's the motive?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late to reply, but this has been reworked to fix this. That phrase "it is believed" is one of those little crutches we both have when skip writing, and it slipped under the radar here. Much better to just say what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complaint is using TSAT's full name as an aic. Doesn't exactly run off the tongue.

That's what you think.

Thank you for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really fun. I'm very pleased that I went through the same logic path of, "It's the director… But what if it's the AI?"

My only complaint is using TSAT's full name as an aic. Doesn't exactly run off the tongue.

item.title: Great Buildup, Middling Conclusion
item.content:encoded:
My first read of this article was post ending-edit. I see some other people in the discussion talking about the O5 voting or something, but I wasn't here for that so I only have the revised ending to go off of.

The buildup was pretty good. I really enjoyed seeing the suspects conversing with each other, even briefly. It did kinda confuse me why they were so resistant to the idea of being contained (even being fairly low-level and low-risk researchers, you would think they would understand how this kind of thing goes), and things escalated a bit too quickly (why did they go from normal, albeit tense, conversations to outright murder attempts? Why isn't a simple blood sample enough?), but overall I still very much enjoyed the buildup. As much as I saw the reveal coming, I still looked forward to seeing how it panned out.

However, as quickly as it accelerated, it decelerated. The O5 note at the end revealing the true nature of SCP-5000 felt rather rushed and tacked on. The article implies it's one person, than implies it's another, and then at the very end just kinda says who it really is with no real fanfare or repercussions. After reading the discussion, I'm not surprised that some things got cut from the ending. I can't speak as to whether it was cut for the better or not, but either way it shows.

Unfortunately, the lackluster ending prevents me from giving a +1, but I still enjoyed my time reading it enough to stick it at a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guessed the twist really early into the entry. it was fishy as hell from the jump that 4 people would basically end up imprisoned based almost entirely on the hunches of an AIC.

that said, it was still a fun read, so +1.

just one tiny thing though:

+ show block
– hide block

"SCP-5000-1 is a contrived thoughtform entity that is believed to have embedded itself amongst Site-608 staff, having fabricated its own memories and history with the Foundation. It is believed that the entity is not malicious, although it is believed that the conflict created by its sudden existence has destabilized reality to an alarming degree, as evident by the discrepancies listed in Addendum 5000.1"

"is believed" shows up three times in the span of two sentences. that's just clunky imo. it could be easily fixed with some synonyms. just a suggestion/example, but something like this: "SCP-5000-1 is a contrived thoughtform entity that is suspected to have embedded itself amongst Site-608 staff, having fabricated its own memories and history with the Foundation. It is believed that the entity is not malicious, although researchers speculate that the conflict created by its sudden existence has destabilized reality to an alarming degree, as evident by the discrepancies listed in Addendum 5000.1."

obviously you dont have to change it in exactly that way, or at all. I'm just kind of a language nerd and those two sentences were grating for me. sorry if that is too nitpicky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

This was great. The characterization was strong, and I didn't see the twist coming. Honestly, this could very well be a play with how dialogue and character-focused it is.

Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This story left me yearning for more the dialogue is easy to read and it has a good twist to it.
This Story has an allowance to add more tales for this SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. It had me always doubting my theories right up to the very end, and yet looking back it had a lot of clever hints toward the true nature of things. Excellent work, thoroughly enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …it was addressed. Danica himself both proposed and enacted this plan when he tried to cut the Junior Researcher. He devised this plan by talking with the resident expert on tulpas. It should be clear from the time stamps that there was not sufficient time to do any more than they have already done before the situation was escalated to violence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorta with Danica's character on this. If it is well known that tulpas don't bleed, wouldn't it be standard operating procedure to just have everyone take a little cut? It feels like that should have been addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. There's some real gems in the dialogue (Larry the Loving Llama comes to mind) and the ending is wonderfully reminiscent of The Thing, which makes it all the more impressive that the only actual gore present in the article is a nosebleed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a pretty conventional old-school Christie-style mystery format, but as a huge fan of such things, that isn't a complaint. This is very well executed, with great dialogue and believable characters, and an excellent twist that doesn't feel cheap. +1

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Originally, we heavily implied that Director Kurth died. Upon second consideration, though, we realized that leaving his fate more ambiguous would more effectively circumvent potential plot holes and logical leaps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PLOTTWIST!

Uh… i need some excuse to write this, so some quick crit: I really, really dig this one. Got an upvote from me. Still, i wouldn't say this is necessarily something belonging to the 5000 slot. I'm afraid, the Scip itself could get "crushed" by the weight of such an important slot.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: But what ended up happening to him? How did the other 4 react when they realized that the thoughtform wasn’t one of them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was heavily inspired by The Thing, at least on my end. RockTeeth has never seen it though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was like a small mystery novel in SCP format and I got really invested in this while reading it. Maybe I should mention one of my favorite movies is The Thing, and at times, that was almost as good as the paranoia-infused scenes from that movie. Didn't see the twist coming, unlike some people in the comments… but maybe I'm just bad at guessing, haha. Upvoted.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: The director was innocent, they all were. TSAT was playing everybody against each other.

I loved it. After the first meeting, I was already going over all the possibilities in my head. TSAT targeting the doctor who knew how it operated was a nice touch. I’m confused about what happened to the Director. Was he a tulpa, or just TSAT?

item.title: Re: No Vote Because Confusion
item.content:encoded: Given this and psul's crit earlier, I think it's become apparent that the O5 vote buys us very little except confounding information. It has been wiped.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

Danica: [gasping for breath] Look at you now! Running back where you came from. With no where else to run.

I'm pretty sure that nowhere is one word.

<End Log08/09/2002, 15:50>

Forgot a comma and space at the end of the final log.

I largely enjoyed the buildup in this article. It was generally realistic, and while I'll second previous comments that there was slightly too much similarity between the characters it was by no means a deal-breaker. I didn't have trouble staying focused through the piece, and I definitely enjoyed the final collapsible quite a bit.

However, I think that the ending is a little disappointing. It was both implied too much and almost too unexpected; the frequency at which tsat was mentioned made me think there was definitely something up with it. However, I feel like it's a little unexpected how the overseer council voted and I'd be lying if I said that I don't fully see its importance to the story. It feels a little like it attempts to fit a larger moral issue into a skip that's not on the correct scale for that sorta thing, being all based on the interactions of four characters prior to this.

Due to my enjoyment of the start but disappointment at the ending, I feel a novote is most suitable here.

P.S: I'd also recommend standardising the dates, possibly to YYYY-MM-DD format, as currently the day and month are ambiguous.

Edit because of course my timing is this bad: Following the removal of the notice, I'm still a little unsure. The ending feels a little empty now, though I do prefer it over the previous. There's not much to it beyond an announcement that it's TSAT, and a description of TSAT's reality altering. It's a neat ah-ha moment, but didn't carry substantial weight for me. I dunno, I might just be disappointed that it was a 'none of you are right' situation, even though I can't think of a member of personnel that I'd put at the thoughtform. I'll give this more thought but my novote is staying for now.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: No Vote Because Confusion
item.content:encoded:
Click to uncollapse
– hide block

baseline non-anomalous humans.

I think you could use baseline or non-anomalous, but both seems redundant.

with 99.5% confidence.

I suggest this be 99.95 since p-values are often 0.05 and are still criticized as being too easy to hack!

Dr. Alan Danica, Dr. Julia Ecker, Junior Researcher Adrian Williams, and Senior Researcher Rafael Hernandez.

I'm not sure how I feel about one title (Dr) being used for specific people and another title (Researcher) being used for other people unless we are implying the researchers don't have PhDs. Which is fine I guess.

that were previously present in SCP-5000

You can cut this.

This office has been furnished with a large wooden desk, two bookshelves, a coffee machine, a filing cabinet, and a desktop computer.

I think this is a tense shift from presence to past and I don't know why unless all the furnishings are gone.

does not have any windows

consider 'contains no windows' in order to avoid the have verb being used twice.

to an alarming degree

This might be a little off tone IMO but not hugely.

Overall I don't know if I like the twist ending. I'm going to have to no-vote at this point in time. I think the thing that's bothering me is I can't discern why the very ending works out the way it does. It's not logical to me, so I don't understand.

EDIT: Ending changed I no longer have concerns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you read a dope 5k entry and see people saying "o h y e a h"

o h y e a h

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed working out the mystery (for which I will give myself half marks), and appreciated the care in the set-up, but the denouement wasn't quite strong enough for me to upvote. A deliberate no-vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shout, been a mad minute since I've been in a Stat class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good dialogue, good twist. The best pacing out of the 5000s I've read (all those above 30+ as of posting). +1.

Stats nerd nitpick:

Subject: Junior Researcher Adrian Williams
Clearance Level: 1
Reported Time Employed by the Foundation: 1 year, 3 months, 14 days
Confidence Interval: 12.3%

"Confidence Interval" makes no sense here; an interval would be something like [10, 12]. A percentage is not an interval! Consider changing it just to "Confidence" - while probably still ill-defined, it wouldn't jump out at me like "Interval" does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved the twist! The dialogue is great and the concept of an .aic being an infiltrant is absolute gold! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've specified further that primary containment detail has been assigned to a division of Nu-7 that I've dubbed the "Echelon Strike Team". Hopefully that makes it clearer that a whole ass army isn't laying siege to Site-608, because that was not our intention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i read this one during crit. though a few of my critiques ended up shaping the final product, it still suffers from sorta samey, messy dialogue and a somewhat (at least to me) predictable twist. i neglected to mention this bit during initial crit because I forgot (my bad), but I found it kinda silly that the foundation would mobilize nu-7, which is an entire goddamn army against this relatively lowkey anomaly.

im iffy on this, so i think im gonna leave it at a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, I guessed the twist immediately. On the other hand, I still enjoyed getting there, so +1.

It's not always about the destination, but the Journey (album that you are listening to on the way).

My main issue with the whole thing: Unless a cognitohazard was involved, I think the researchers would have.. You know.. Just cut him a bit to see what came out, rather than going full blown silence of the lambs.

I can see how that'd be an issue. I personally feel that the dramatic nature of it is justified. Director had failed to provide substantial information, got upset that people disregarded his authority, and responded by assaulting a member of his personnel. The degree to which they maintained their defensiveness only made him more suspicious.

In any case, I'm glad to hear that you still liked it enough to upvote. Thank you for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, I guessed the twist immediately. On the other hand, I still enjoyed getting there, so +1.

My main issue with the whole thing: Unless a cognitohazard was involved, I think the researchers would have.. You know.. Just cut him a bit to see what came out, rather than going full blown silence of the lambs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you've been critting things and you see them on the main site:

o h y e a h

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you've been lurking on sandboxes and you see the entries on the main site:

o h y e a h

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, that was a good one. +1

item.title: Also an Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm fucking sick of all the lies and all the treachery
I was always by your side and this is how you repay me
And after all these years you fucking turned your back on me
I can't believe I ever considered you family
I will never forget this knife you placed in my back

Recreant ~ Chelsea Grin

Welcome to the McRockteeth 5kcon Extravaganza! This was a lot of fun to write, and it's my first time participating in a Kcon! Shout out to RockTeethMothEyes for being a straight up awesome partner!

PS: Just noting for posterity that Addendum 5000.4 is a new addition that has been made as per some criticism that we've received from Nico and Kirby. Appreciate the thoughts, guys, I think the article is better for it now.

Special thanks to our critters:
N_Aepic_Fael, plaidypus, MalyceGraves, rattles, XilasCrowe, stormbreath, and Uncle Nicolini

Click Here for more stuff by me!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Trust No One

A MightyMcRockTeeth Production.

Thank you to our reviewers as listed below by TheMightyMcB. This collaboration was a joy to make and I'm so glad to have had McB as a partner.

And of course, thank you for reading. May the best participant win. Contest is over! We finished in 12th place! :D You may now recognize this piece as it's true name: SCP-5006.

Did you like this? You did?! Then might I show you to my other works?
